title: Keyword-arg formals (#:name) unusable in ANY file that also contains a dotted (require ...) statement -- __keyword-arg/__keyword-args-validate unresolved
goal: Discovered while implementing ESH-0196 (P11 tower-based user numerics). A file that both (a) uses a keyword-arg formal, e.g. `(define (f x #:scale s) ...)`, and (b) contains a dotted-module `(require core.something)` statement ANYWHERE in the same file (order does not matter -- before or after the kwarg definition), fails to compile with `Unknown function: __keyword-arg` / `Unknown function: __keyword-args-validate`, both under -r and AOT. Root cause is presumably that keyword-formal desugaring (lib/frontend/parser.cpp wrap_keyword_formal_body, ~line 989-1027) expands to calls to __keyword-arg/__keyword-args-validate, which are themselves plain Scheme functions defined in lib/stdlib.esk (lines ~86-102) -- and processing a dotted `require` appears to rebuild/replace the compiler's known-function symbol table in a way that drops stdlib.esk's own definitions for the remainder of that compilation unit.
repro: (define (f x #:scale s) (* x s)) (display (f 2 #:scale 3)) -- compiles and runs fine standalone (prints 6).

(require core.list.search) (define (f x #:scale s) (* x s)) (display (f 2 #:scale 3)) -- FAILS: Unknown function: __keyword-arg / __keyword-args-validate, even though core.list.search is ALREADY transitively required by stdlib.esk itself (i.e. this is not a case of requiring something exotic).
Same failure with (require core.ad.guw), (require core.ad.tape), or any other dotted-name require target that resolves under the lib search path.

Requiring a module via an explicit PATH LITERAL (e.g. a bare .esk path or one starting with / or ./) instead of a dotted name does NOT trigger the failure -- resolveModulePath (lib/repl/repl_jit.cpp ~2616) treats path literals and dotted names via different branches, which may be the relevant fork.
Placing the require BEFORE or AFTER the kwarg-formal definition makes no difference -- both fail identically, ruling out a simple textual-order/forward-reference explanation.
acceptance: A file that requires any dotted core/stdlib module AND defines/calls a keyword-arg-formal function compiles and runs correctly under both -r and AOT, with the same semantics as a file with no require at all
Existing keyword_args_test.esk and every currently-passing dotted-require-using test continue to pass unchanged
A regression test combining both features in one file is added (e.g. tests/features/keyword_args_with_require_test.esk)
workaround_in_place: lib/core/ad/taylor_numerics.esk (ESH-0196) avoids keyword-arg formals entirely, using plain positional arguments instead of the design doc's #:order/#:steps sketch. tests/ad/taylor_numerics_test.esk (which requires that module) also avoids list-ref (see ESH-0189's guw_multivariate_test.esk precedent) via a local `nth` helper, since list-ref hits the exact same underlying symbol-resolution loss. This workaround is no longer NECESSARY after the fix below, but was left in place on the P11 branch since that branch is out of scope for this fix.
related: ESH-0189 (guw.esk/guw_multivariate_test.esk already work around list-ref the same way, likely the same root cause, just never diagnosed as a require-triggered issue before)
root_cause: NOT a symbol-table rebuild/replace as originally suspected. exe/eshkol-run.cpp's AOT driver only pulls in stdlib.esk's own top-level definitions (__keyword-arg, __keyword-args-validate, __keyword-member? -- none of which are re-exported by any individual core.* submodule's `provide`, since stdlib.esk itself has no `provide` at all) by synthesizing a top-of-module `(require stdlib)` AST node when the user's file doesn't already look like it handles its own stdlib needs (see the `need_stdlib` block, originally around line 4014). That gate was `if (need_stdlib && !requires_stdlib(asts))`, and requires_stdlib(asts) returns true for EITHER an explicit `(require stdlib)` OR any dotted `(require core.*)` -- so a file with an explicit `(require core.list.search)` (or any other dotted core.* require) but NO explicit `(require stdlib)` looked, to that gate, like it already 'requires stdlib', and the synthetic require was skipped entirely. Only the explicitly-named core.* module's own declarations got pulled in as externs -- stdlib.esk's own top-level helpers never did, so `__keyword-arg`/`__keyword-args-validate` were simply never declared for that compilation unit. -r/JIT mode was unaffected because the REPL JIT discovers stdlib.bc's full symbol set at startup regardless of what the source file requires, which is exactly why the bug was invisible under -r and only bit under AOT.
fix: exe/eshkol-run.cpp: added a new, narrower `requires_stdlib_module_explicitly(asts)` helper that ONLY matches an explicit `(require stdlib)` (no core.* fallback), and changed the `need_stdlib` gate to use it instead of the broad `requires_stdlib(asts)`. `requires_stdlib(asts)` itself is unchanged and still used (correctly) to decide whether stdlib.o needs to be auto-linked at all -- that decision legitimately should fire on any core.* require, since core.* module bodies live in stdlib.o. The two checks were conflated under one function; splitting them is the root fix, not a workaround: it makes 'does this file need stdlib.esk's own top-level definitions synthesized' independent from 'does this file need the stdlib.o object linked'.
verification: Repro from the ticket confirmed failing pre-fix under AOT only (`Unknown function: __keyword-arg` / `__keyword-args-validate`; -r/JIT was never affected, see root_cause) and passing post-fix under both -r and AOT. Full regression: tests/features (27/27, includes existing keyword_args_test.esk and the new keyword_args_require_test.esk), tests/modules (9/9), ICC keyword_args_work probe still green.
deliverable_test: tests/features/keyword_args_require_test.esk -- dotted require before a keyword-arg define, reordered keyword-arg calls, a keyword formal defined AFTER the require, a Scheme-level default-via-rest-scan pattern for an omitted optional keyword, and a lambda (not just define) keyword formal, all alongside `(require core.list.search)`.
